mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Tunisia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Africa, on the north coast. Tunisia faces the Mediterranean, and Sicily is only 140 km across the water 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Tunisia?
mapQuestions.answer: Tunis. The old part of Tunis (the "medina") is one of the largest and best-preserved medieval Arab old towns in the world, a UNESCO World Heritage site.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Tunisia speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Arabic is the official language, French is widely spoken (from the colonial era), and some Berber communities still speak Tamazight, especially in the south.

mapQuestions.question: What ancient city was in Tunisia?
mapQuestions.answer: Carthage, which sat just outside modern Tunis. Carthage was Rome's GREATEST rival for hundreds of years, with its general Hannibal famously crossing the Alps on war elephants to attack Rome in 218 BC.

mapQuestions.question: How is Tunisia linked to Star Wars?
mapQuestions.answer: Most of the desert scenes on Luke Skywalker's home planet "Tatooine" in Star Wars were filmed in Tunisia. The name "Tatooine" comes from the real Tunisian town of Tataouine!

foods.name: Couscous
foods.description: Tiny steamed semolina grains, served with lamb, vegetables and harissa. Tunisia, Algeria and Morocco all claim couscous, and each version is slightly different. The national Friday dish here too.

foods.name: Brik
foods.description: A thin paper-like pastry folded over a whole raw egg, capers, tuna and parsley, then deep-fried until the pastry crisps and the egg stays just runny in the middle. Tricky to eat without spilling.

foods.name: Harissa
foods.description: A fiery red chilli paste made with sun-dried peppers, garlic, caraway, coriander and olive oil. Tunisians put harissa on EVERYTHING. The national condiment.

foods.name: Lablabi
foods.description: A breakfast chickpea soup with cumin, garlic, olive oil, lemon, harissa and a poached egg, served over torn-up day-old bread in a bowl. Cheap, warm and surprisingly addictive.

foods.name: Mloukhia
foods.description: A dark green slow-cooked meat stew (made with finely chopped jute leaves), eaten with bread, mostly cooked for special family meals. Looks unusual, tastes deep and warming.

foods.name: Makroudh
foods.description: Diamond-shaped semolina pastries stuffed with date paste, deep-fried and dipped in honey syrup. Tunisia, Algeria and Morocco all make their own slightly different version.

scavengerItems.label: The blue-and-white painted houses of Sidi Bou Said clifftop village
Ancient Roman or Punic ruins (Carthage, Dougga, El Jem, Sufetula)
A "medina" (the old walled town centre, narrow alleys, lanterns, doors)
A Star Wars filming location (Mos Espa film set, Matmata)
A camel and a Berber guide on the edge of the Sahara
Olive trees stretching to the horizon (Tunisia is in the world's top 5 olive-oil producers)
Harissa being made or sold by the spoonful in a market
A "ksar" (fortified Berber granary, the inspiration for many Tatooine sets)
The El Jem amphitheatre (one of the biggest Roman amphitheatres outside Rome)
A Matmata "troglodyte" home (carved underground into the rock)
Sidi Bou Said cats lounging in plant pots and blue doorways

geoMatches.description: The capital and biggest city, on the north coast. The medina is a UNESCO World Heritage site with hidden palaces, covered souks and mosaic-tiled mosques.
The ancient city just outside Tunis, founded by Phoenician settlers around 800 BC. For centuries it was Rome's biggest rival. Ruins, baths and a harbour are still visible.
The famous blue-and-white clifftop village just along the coast from Tunis. Painters, writers and tourists have loved it for over a hundred years.
A small inland town with a colossal Roman amphitheatre rising out of nowhere, third-biggest in the Roman world, holding 35,000 spectators.
A village in the south where Berber families have lived for centuries in homes dug DOWN into the ground, with rooms around an open pit. Some are Star Wars filming locations.
A flat sandy island in the south-east Mediterranean. The legendary "land of the lotus eaters" from Homer's Odyssey, with one of Africa's oldest synagogues.

senses.smell: Hot olive oil with garlic, cumin and coriander frying in a pan, jasmine pinned to people's ears on summer evenings, salt and bougainvillea in Sidi Bou Said, fresh harissa being ground, the dry sun-baked smell of the Sahara…
senses.hear: Arabic and French swapped mid-sentence in cafés, the call to prayer drifting across rooftops, "Labas?" and "Behi!" between friends, the click of dominoes in a souk café, the wind across Sahara dunes…
senses.taste: Spicy red harissa on bread, crispy brik with runny egg, slow-cooked couscous, sweet honey-soaked makroudh, mint tea poured from high up to make a froth…
senses.feel: Hot Mediterranean sun on white-washed walls, smooth carved-stone Roman ruins, soft yellow Sahara sand running through your fingers, cool tile floors in a riad courtyard, the warm Mediterranean Sea…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Who was Hannibal, and did he really cross the Alps on elephants?
stories.body: Yes! Hannibal Barca was a general from Carthage (just outside modern Tunis) who, in 218 BC, decided that the best way to attack the Roman Empire was the most unexpected route: marching his entire army OVERLAND through Spain, across southern France, and OVER the Alps into Italy. With him went around 50,000 soldiers, 9,000 horsemen, and 37 war elephants. The Alpine crossing was a disaster: snow, cliffs, frostbite, and most of the elephants died. But Hannibal made it down into Italy with the rest and went on to win some of the most famous battles in history. The Romans eventually beat him, and 60 years later they sacked Carthage so completely they ploughed salt into the ground so nothing would grow there. Carthage was eventually rebuilt and survives in ruins today.

stories.question: How is Star Wars connected to Tunisia?
stories.body: When George Lucas needed a desert planet for Luke Skywalker's home in Star Wars (1977), he picked Tunisia. The wide pale dunes, the strange-looking Berber underground homes of Matmata, and the white-domed ksour (granaries) in the south were used directly as Tatooine. Luke's family kitchen was filmed inside a real Matmata cave home (the Hotel Sidi Driss, still open as a hotel today, with the original set bits left as decor). The dusty desert town "Mos Espa" was built as a film set near Tozeur and is still standing in the dunes as a tourist site, even though the sand is slowly burying it. Lucas even named the planet "Tatooine" after the real Tunisian town of Tataouine, just down the road.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A Hollywood film borrowed a real place and turned it into an imaginary planet. Has a movie you love ever made you want to visit somewhere real?

stories.question: How big is Tunisian olive oil?
stories.body: Tunisia is one of the top five olive-oil producers on Earth. The country has more than 80 MILLION olive trees, which is about 7 trees for every person. Some Tunisian olive groves are over 2,000 years old, planted in Roman times and still bearing olives today. In some southern regions, you can drive for an hour and see almost nothing except olive trees lined up to the horizon. Olive oil is poured liberally on couscous, brik, lablabi, and used as the base of harissa. Tunisia exports its olive oil to Italy, Spain and France, where some of it ends up being labelled as Italian or Spanish.

stories.question: Why is there a giant Colosseum in a small Tunisian town?
stories.body: In a small inland town called El Jem, a 35,000-seat Roman amphitheatre rises straight out of the houses. It is the third-largest amphitheatre in the entire Roman world (only Rome itself and Capua were bigger), and one of the best-preserved Roman monuments anywhere. It was built in the early 3rd century AD when this region (then called "Africa Proconsularis") was one of Rome's richest, exporting olive oil, grain and wild animals. The amphitheatre was used for the same gladiator fights and animal shows you would see in Rome's Colosseum. Today it sometimes hosts opera festivals in summer.

stories.question: Why are Tunisian medinas so important?
stories.body: A "medina" is the old walled centre of an Arab city. Tunisia has some of the best-preserved medinas in the world, in Tunis, Sousse, Sfax and Kairouan. Inside the walls are narrow shaded alleys, hidden courtyards, covered markets ("souks") grouped by trade (the perfume souk, the shoe souk, the gold souk), fountains, hammams (Turkish baths), and mosques. The medina of Tunis dates back to the 7th century AD and is a UNESCO World Heritage site. Walking into a medina is like stepping out of the modern city and into a city laid out 1,300 years ago by people who designed the streets so that they would stay cool in summer and busy with talk.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Tunisia so far?
Have you tried saying "shukran" or "labas" yet? What was the answer?
Mediterranean coast, Sahara, ancient ruins, medina, which has felt the most "Tunisia"?
Did you spot a Star Wars filming location?
On a 1 to 10 scale, how hot is the harissa you have tried so far?
Tunisia has Roman ruins, Arab medinas, Berber villages, and French streets, all in one country. How does that feel as you move between them?

wordSearch.hint: Tunisian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Medina cat
animals.description: Cats lounging in plant pots, on warm blue tiles, in every Sidi Bou Said and Tunis doorway.

animals.name: Fennec fox
animals.description: The same big-eared desert fox as in Algeria. In the southern Tunisian Sahara.

animals.name: Dromedary camel
animals.description: In the south, used for tourist rides into the dunes and for working in the oases.

animals.name: Greater flamingo
animals.description: Big flocks at the salt lakes of Ichkeul and the lagoons near Djerba.

animals.name: White stork
animals.description: Storks nest on the tops of minarets and ruined Roman columns across the north.

animals.name: Barbary deer
animals.description: A small reddish deer found in the cork-oak forests of the far north. Endangered.

animals.name: Eurasian otter
animals.description: Returning to the lakes and rivers of the Tunisian Atlas mountains.
